---
title: "Inventory Insights"
---

The tree inventory reveals distinct patterns in tree age and distribution across the study area. Large mature trees are primarily concentrated within Kells Park, indicating a long-established canopy in that location. In contrast, newly planted trees are most common in JH-P, reflecting recent restoration or expansion efforts. The following figures summarize key insights into tree density, size, and condition, highlighting both the spatial distribution of significant trees and the health trends across different sites.

## Tree Inventory Map 

```{python}
#| echo: false
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

import pandas as pd

trees = pd.read_csv("data/processedTreeData.csv")

fig = go.Figure()

# Color set
colors = (
    px.colors.qualitative.Plotly +
    px.colors.qualitative.Pastel
)

sites = ["RH-P"]

for i, site in enumerate(sites):
    site_trees = trees[trees["Site"] == site]
    legend_added = False   # Track legend per site

    # Large mature trees
    large_trees = site_trees[
        site_trees["Type of Significant Trees"] == "Large mature tree"
    ]

    if not large_trees.empty:
        fig.add_trace(
            go.Scattermap(
                lat=large_trees["Latitude"],
                lon=large_trees["Longitude"],
                mode="markers",
                name=site,
                legendgroup=site,
                showlegend=not legend_added,
                marker=dict(
                    size=large_trees["DBH"]*.5,
                    opacity=0.8,
                    color=colors[i % len(colors)]
                ),
                text=(large_trees["Site"].astype(str) + "-" +
                      large_trees["Tree ID"].astype(str).str.zfill(3)),
                customdata=large_trees[[
                    "DBH", "Tree Height", "Tree Condition"
                ]],
                hovertemplate=
                    "<b>%{text}</b><br>" +
                    "Diameter at Breast Height (dbh): %{customdata[0]}<br>" +
                    "Height: %{customdata[1]}<br>" +
                    "Condition: %{customdata[2]}<br>" +
                    "<extra></extra>"
            )
        )
        legend_added = True

    # Small trees
    small_trees = site_trees[
        site_trees["Type of Significant Trees"].isin(
            ["Young tree", "Newly planted tree"]
        )
    ]

    if not small_trees.empty:
        fig.add_trace(
            go.Scattermap(
                lat=small_trees["Latitude"],
                lon=small_trees["Longitude"],
                mode="markers",
                name=site,
                legendgroup=site,
                showlegend=not legend_added,   # Only if legend not yet created
                marker=dict(
                    size=small_trees["DBH"]*3,
                    opacity=0.8,
                    color=colors[i % len(colors)]
                ),
                text=(small_trees["Site"].astype(str) + "-" +
                      small_trees["Tree ID"].astype(str).str.zfill(3)),
                customdata=small_trees[[
                    "DBH", "Tree Height", "Tree Condition"
                ]],
                hovertemplate=
                    "<b>%{text}</b><br>" +
                    "Diameter at Breast Height (dbh): %{customdata[0]}<br>" +
                    "Height: %{customdata[1]}<br>" +
                    "Condition: %{customdata[2]}<br>" +
                    "<extra></extra>"
            )
        )
        legend_added = True



fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text="Tree Inventory Map",
        x=0.5,
        xanchor='center',
        font=dict(size=15)
    ),
    map=dict(
        style="carto-positron",
        zoom=14,
        center=dict(
            lat=trees["Latitude"].mean(),
            lon=trees["Longitude"].mean()
        )
    ),
    margin=dict(r=0, t=30, l=0, b=0),
    height=600
)

fig

```
<br/>
**Figure 3.** Tree inventory map. Each point is an inventoried tree with its size proportional to its DBH. For the sake of visualization, large mature trees are scaled by 0.5 , while young and newly planted trees are scaled by 3.  

## Tree Density


```{python}
#| echo: false
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=DeprecationWarning)
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)

import plotly.express as px

# Map center
center_lat = trees["Latitude"].mean()
center_lon = trees["Longitude"].mean()

fig = px.density_mapbox(
    trees,
    lat="Latitude",
    lon="Longitude",
    radius=10,
    center=dict(
        lat=trees["Latitude"].mean(),
        lon=trees["Longitude"].mean()
    ),
    zoom=13.5,
    mapbox_style="carto-positron"
)

fig.update_layout(
    coloraxis_colorbar=dict(
        title="Tree Count (Relative Density)"
    )
)

fig.show()

```
<br/>
**Figure 4.** Tree density map (Lighter areas indicate higher number of trees).

## Tree Height, DBH, and Average Spread Distribution
```{python}
#| echo: false
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Metrics to plot
metrics = [
    ("Tree Height", "Tree Height"),
    ("DBH", "DBH"),
    ("Average Spread", "Average Spread")
]

tree_types = ["Large mature tree", "Young tree", "Newly planted tree"]

# Custom color map by type of significant trees
type_colors = {
    "Newly planted tree": "#D7CCC8",   # soft beige/light brown
    "Young tree": "#81C784",           # medium green
    "Large mature tree": "#388E3C"     # forest green
}

# Create subplots
fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1,
    cols=3,
    subplot_titles=[m[0] for m in metrics]
)

# Add initial traces (first tree type)
initial_type = tree_types[0]

for col, (_, column_name) in enumerate(metrics, start=1):
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Histogram(
            x=trees.loc[
                trees["Type of Significant Trees"] == initial_type,
                column_name
            ],
            nbinsx=15,
            marker_color=type_colors[initial_type],
            showlegend=False
        ),
        row=1,
        col=col
    )

# Dropdown buttons
buttons = []

for ttype in tree_types:
    buttons.append(
        dict(
            label=ttype,
            method="update",
            args=[
                {
                    "x": [
                        trees.loc[
                            trees["Type of Significant Trees"] == ttype,
                            col_name
                        ]
                        for _, col_name in metrics
                    ],
                    "marker.color": [type_colors[ttype]] * len(metrics)
                }
            ]
        )
    )

# Layout updates
fig.update_layout(
    updatemenus=[
        dict(
            buttons=buttons,
            direction="down",
            showactive=True,
            x=0.5,
            y=1.2,          # moved higher
            xanchor="center",
            yanchor="top"
        )
    ],
    bargap=0.2,
    height=450
)

# Axis labels
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Value")
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="Count")

fig

```
<br/>
**Figure 5.** Histogram of tree height, DBH, average spread for each type of signficiant trees. Toggle to switch between each type of trees. 

## Tree Type Distribution Across Sites
<br/>
```{python}
#| echo: false

counts_bar = (
    trees.groupby(["Site", "Type of Significant Trees"])
         .size()
         .reset_index(name="Count")
)

# Order sites by total number of trees (descending)
site_order = (
    counts_bar.groupby("Site")["Count"]
              .sum()
              .sort_values(ascending=False)
              .index
              .tolist()
)

# ORDERING of type of significant tree & site location
# Define the desired order for tree types
tree_order = ["Large mature tree", "Young tree", "Newly planted tree"]

# PREPARING the pie chart data
counts_pie = trees["Type of Significant Trees"].value_counts().reindex(tree_order, fill_value=0)
total_trees = counts_pie.sum()

# Creating the subplots
fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1,
    cols=2,
    column_widths=[0.4, 0.6],
    horizontal_spacing=0.18,  # Increased to prevent squishing
    specs=[[{"type": "domain"}, {"type": "bar"}]],
    subplot_titles=("A. Overall Type of Signficiant Trees", "B. Type of Signficiant Trees by Site")
)

# After creating the subplots
for ann in fig['layout']['annotations']:
    # Only adjust subplot titles (not main title)
    ann['y'] += 0.02  # move up

# Add pie chart with no legend
fig.add_trace(
    go.Pie(
        labels=counts_pie.index,
        values=counts_pie.values,
        hole=0.45,
        marker_colors=[type_colors[t] for t in counts_pie.index],
        textinfo="percent",
        texttemplate="%{percent:.2%}",   # <-- format with 2 decimals
        textposition="inside",
        hovertemplate="%{label}: %{value} trees (%{percent})<extra></extra>",
        showlegend=False
    ),
    row=1,
    col=1
)

# Add stacked bar chart
for tree_type in tree_order:  # ensures correct stack order
    df = counts_bar[counts_bar["Type of Significant Trees"] == tree_type]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Bar(
            x=df["Count"],
            y=df["Site"],
            name=tree_type,
            orientation="h",
            marker_color=type_colors[tree_type],
            text=df["Count"],
            textposition="inside",
            insidetextanchor="middle"
        ),
        row=1,
        col=2
    )


# Pie annotation
pie_domain = fig.data[0].domain  # first trace is the pie

x_center = sum(pie_domain.x) / 2
y_center = sum(pie_domain.y) / 2

fig.add_annotation(
    text=f"<b>{total_trees}</b><br>Trees",
    x=(x_center-0.03),
    y=y_center,
    xref="paper",
    yref="paper",
    showarrow=False,
    font_size=20
)



fig.update_layout(
    barmode="stack",
    xaxis_title="Count",
    yaxis_title="Site Location",
    yaxis=dict(
        categoryorder="array",
        categoryarray=site_order[::-1]  # <-- reverse the order here
    ),
    legend_traceorder="normal", # Proper legend order
    legend=dict(
        orientation="h",
        yanchor="top",
        y=-0.1,
        xanchor="right",
        x=1
    ),
    height=600
)

fig.show()


```
<br/>
**Figure 6.** Type of signficiant trees overall and by site location. 

Overall, the majority of the inventoried trees are large and mature (72.15%), as illustrated by Figure [6A](inventoryInsights.html#tree-type-distribution-across-sites). This indicates that the trail and parks are dominated by established mature trees rather than recent plantings.

As shown in Figure [6B](inventoryInsights.html#tree-type-distribution-across-sites), Kell Park contains the highest number of significant trees (30), followed closely by JH-W (29), while Lewis Park and Philips Park each have 21 significant trees. Together, park spaces account for nearly half of all inventoried trees (45.57%).

Figure [6B](inventoryInsights.html#tree-type-distribution-across-sites) further shows that Kell Park has the greatest concentration of large mature trees (25), making it the site with the greatest number of high-value trees for ongoing monitoring and protection. Young trees were recorded only in Philips Park and JH-P, with JH-P also having the highest number of newly planted trees, highlighting these sites as priorities for establishment and long-term survivability.

## Multi-Branch Trees Across Sites
```{python}
#| echo: false
counts = (
    trees.groupby(["Site", "Multi-branch?"])
         .size()
         .reset_index(name="Count")
)

diverge_colors = {
    "No": "#388E3C",     # forest green
    "Yes": "#EF9A9A"
}
fig = px.bar(
    counts,
    x="Count",
    y="Site",
    color="Multi-branch?",
    color_discrete_map=diverge_colors, # <-- color mapping
    orientation="h",
    barmode="stack",
    category_orders={
        "Site": site_order                         # sets y-axis order
    }
)


# Put labels INSIDE every bar segment
fig.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{x}",   # show the actual count
    textposition="inside",
    insidetextanchor="middle"
)


fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Count",
    yaxis_title="Site Location",
    legend_title="Multi-branch?",
    height=600
)

fig.show()
```
<br/>
**Figure 7.** Overall number of multi-branch trees by site.

<br/>

| Site | Large mature tree | Young tree | Newly planted tree |
|------|-------------------|------------|--------------------|
| JH-W | 4 | 0 | 0 |
| JH-P | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| JH-A | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| PHIL | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| LEW  | 6 | 0 | 0 |
| KELL | 8 | 0 | 1 |

<br/>

**Table 2.** Count of multi-branch trees by site and significant tree type, excluding sites with no inventoried multi-branch trees.

Multi-branch trees provide greater canopy coverage, as multiple branches contribute to foliage and shade. Among the inventoried multi-branch trees, some may be codominant stems, where two or more large stems grow from the same point, forming a narrow “V”-shaped crotch. These stems can be structurally weaker as the tree matures and are most effectively managed through pruning while the trees are young ([Mitchell 2017](inventoryInsights.html#references)).

As shown in Figure [7](inventoryInsights.html#multi-branch-trees-across-sites), Kells Park contains the highest number of multi-branch trees (9). Of these, eight are classified as large mature trees (Table [2](inventoryInsights.html#multi-branch-trees-across-sites)), suggesting that this site would benefit from focused monitoring over time.

In addition, multi-branch young and newly planted trees were identified in JH-P, Philips Park, and Kells Park, where early pruning may help guide branch development and reduce future maintenance needs.

## Orthogonal Spread Measurements

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

colors = px.colors.qualitative.Plotly + px.colors.qualitative.Pastel

# Build customdata for hover
customdata = trees[["Site", "Tree ID", "Spread 1", "Spread 2"]]

# Scatter plot
fig = px.scatter(
    trees,
    x="Spread 1",
    y="Spread 2",
    color="Site",                  # categorical coloring
    color_discrete_sequence=colors,
    custom_data=customdata
)

# Update hover template to show Site-TreeID + Spread 1/2
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate=(
        "<b>%{customdata[0]}-%{customdata[1]:03}</b><br>" +
        "Spread 1: %{customdata[2]}<br>" +
        "Spread 2: %{customdata[3]}<br>" +
        "<extra></extra>"
    )
)

# Add y = x line
x_min = trees[["Spread 1", "Spread 2"]].min().min()
x_max = trees[["Spread 1", "Spread 2"]].max().max()
buffer = (x_max - x_min) * 0.05
x_line = np.linspace(x_min - buffer, x_max + buffer, 200)

fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=x_line,
        y=x_line,
        mode="lines",
        name="y = x",
        line=dict(dash="dash", color="black")
    )
)

# Axis labels and legend
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Spread 1",
    yaxis_title="Spread 2",
    legend=dict(
        orientation="v",
        yanchor="top",
        y=1,
        xanchor="left",
        x=1.02,          # outside the plot
        title_text="Site"
    ),
    margin=dict(l=60, r=120, t=80, b=60)  # expand right margin
)

fig.show()
```
<br/>
**Figure 8.** Spread was measured in two perpendicular directions. This graph shows the first spread measurement against the second spread measurement for each site. Hover each point to see more information.

A large difference between orthogonal spread measurements indicates a highly irregular, asymmetrical canopy. This suggests the tree is not growing evenly and may be crowded by neighboring trees.

## Tree Conditions 

```{python}
#| echo: false

#colors for conditions
COND_COLORS = {
    "Good": "#00CC96",  # green
    "Fair": "#636EFA",  # blue
    "Poor": "#EF553B",  # red
}

COND_ORDER = ["Good", "Fair", "Poor"]

counts = (
    trees.groupby(["Tree Condition", "Type of Significant Trees"])
         .size()
         .reset_index(name="count")
)

fig9 = px.bar(
    counts,
    x="Type of Significant Trees",
    y="count",
    color="Tree Condition",
    barmode="stack",
    labels={
        "Type of Significant Trees": "Type of Significant Tree",
        "count": "Number of Trees",
        "tree Condition": "Tree Condition"
    },
    category_orders={"Tree Condition": COND_ORDER},
    color_discrete_map=COND_COLORS
)

fig9.show()
```
<br/>

**Figure 9.** Tree condition breakdown by type of significant tree. Poor condition trees are all large and mature.

```{python}
#| echo: false
loc_counts = (
    trees.groupby(["Site", "Tree Condition"])
         .size()
         .reset_index(name="count")
)

fig11 = px.bar(
    loc_counts,
    x="Site",
    y="count",
    color="Tree Condition",
    barmode="stack",
    labels={
        "Site": "Location",
        "count": "Number of Trees",
        "Tree Condition": "Tree Condition"
    },
    category_orders={"Tree Condition": COND_ORDER},
    color_discrete_map=COND_COLORS
)

fig11.show()
```

**Figure 10.** Condition of trees by site.

Across all inventoried trees, the majority (81.65%) were rated in good condition, with smaller proportions classified as fair (8.86%) or poor (9.49%). 

Tree condition was not evenly distributed across site locations. Notably, JH-W had the highest number of poor-condition trees (10), which stands out relative to most other locations.

When toggling on only poor-condition trees in Figure [10](inventoryInsights.html#tree-conditions), trail locations (JH-A, JH-L, JH-UD, and JH-W) collectively accounted for a substantially higher number of poor-condition trees (13) compared to park locations (LEW and PHIL), which together contained only two. This finding suggests that trees along trails may be more vulnerable to stressors than those in park settings.

<div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="/assets/poorConditionWordCloud.png" width="750">
</div>

**Figure 11.** Word cloud showing the most commonly noted characteristics of poor-condition trees. Visual rotting is the most commonly noted characteristics of poor-condition trees.

## References 
Mitchell, Ralph E. “Codominant Stems – Potential Future Tree Failures.” UF/IFAS Extension Charlotte County, 18 May 2017, blogs.ifas.ufl.edu/charlotteco/2017/05/18/codominant-stems-potential-future-tree-failures/.
